title: California creates $3,500 rebate for new electric vehicle buyers
gist: California launched a state-funded MyFirstEV rebate ($3,500 new/$1,750 used) to replace the federal IRS EV tax credit that Trump-era policy eliminated last September, but the program exempts California-headquartered automakers like Rivian and Lucid from its price cap while Tesla (now Texas-based) does not get that exemption.

themes.theme: State EV incentives replace federal credit

insights.claim: The elimination of the IRS Section 30D federal EV tax credit (up to $7,500) at the end of last September caused US EV sales to drop sharply and led automakers to cancel entire EV product lines.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Confirms federal policy withdrawal is a material headwind for US EV demand and OEM product roadmaps; investors should expect continued EV volume softness outside states with offsetting incentives.
insights.quote: Since then, EV sales have dried up, and automakers have canceled entire product lines

insights.claim: California's new MyFirstEV program offers a $3,500 point-of-sale rebate for first-time buyers of new EVs under $50,000, and a $1,750 rebate for used EVs under $25,000, funded by $135.5 million from the state budget matched by $135.5 million in automaker contributions.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: A concrete, funded state-level demand subsidy partly offsets the loss of the federal credit for California buyers, supporting EV sales volume in that single largest US auto market even as national demand weakens.

insights.claim: California-headquartered EV manufacturers such as Rivian and Lucid are exempt from the $50,000 price cap under the MyFirstEV rebate, giving them an incentive advantage over Tesla, which moved its headquarters to Texas and is therefore subject to the cap.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: A state policy carve-out effectively rewards Rivian and Lucid's higher-priced EV lineups with unrestricted rebate eligibility while capping Tesla's benefit to sub-$50k models, a modest but real competitive tilt among California-linked automakers.
insights.quote: As long as an EV manufacturer is headquartered in the state—like Rivian and Lucid—its products are not subject to the price cap.
